result.rationale: Root cause is correct: _resolve_locked_param(None, None) returns None (verified), so an unlocked/unsupplied alpha previously hit `0.0 < None` in _validate_alpha and raised an opaque TypeError. The fix adds a None guard at the top of _validate_alpha (confirm.py:87-91) — the single validation chokepoint reached by verdict_from_pvalue, confirm_whole_corpus, and (via verdict_from_pvalue) confirm_unit_level — raising a ValueError that names alpha and states both ways to provide it. Right layer, sound approach. The regression test (tests/test_confirm.py:132) asserts pytest.raises(ValueError, match='alpha is required'); discrimination verified empirically: simulating the revert restores TypeError, which pytest.raises(ValueError) does NOT catch, so the test fails on revert. Full suite 45/45 passes, including valid-alpha and boundary cases. P9-MO-1 is a pure-logic fix; the CI-1 constraint does not apply.

The fix addresses the correct root cause at the right layer. In src/assay_engine/_local.py, both the DSN keyword branch (lines 167-178) and the URI query branch (lines 154-164) now SPLIT host/hostaddr values on "," and validate EVERY element with is_loopback_host OR is_local_socket_path. is_local_socket_path() also fails closed when a comma is present (lines 56-57) as defense in depth. Empirical verification of all required vectors passed: require_local_uri REJECTS 'host=/tmp,evil.com', 'host=/var/run/postgresql,[REDACTED]', and 'postgresql:///db?host=/var/run/postgresql,evil.com', plus '//evil/share'; and ACCEPTS single-host 'host=/var/run/postgresql', 'host=/tmp', 'postgresql://localhost/db', 'host=127.0.0.1'. The test suite (test_require_local_uri_rejects_multihost_socket_bypass plus the accept and UNC tests) passes (9 passed). Discrimination verified by reverting: reverting only the socket-guard comma check leaves the test green (caller splits still catch it, correct DiD behavior), but reverting the caller-side per-element split (the load-bearing fix) makes all 4 bypass cases DID NOT RAISE and the test fails — so the test genuinely fails when the fix is reverted. File restored clean afterward.

Fix is appropriate AND complete. Root cause was that the pass-8 leading-"/" socket allowance ran on the WHOLE host value, so libpq's comma-separated multi-host form (socket THEN off-box TCP fallback) waved through an off-box host after the comma. The fix in /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/_local.py is at the correct layer (require_local_uri, the data-sovereignty guard) and addresses the root cause directly: (1) is_local_socket_path() now returns False on any comma (defense-in-depth leaf, lines 56-58); (2) the DSN keyword/value branch splits host/hostaddr on "," and validates EVERY element with is_loopback_host OR is_local_socket_path (lines 167-178); (3) the URI query branch does the same for host/hostaddr params (lines 154-164); (4) the authority branch already handled multi-host via _authority_hosts (#D3). Empirical verification matched the spec exactly: all 3 (plus a 4th in-test) bypass DSNs REJECTED — host=/tmp,evil.com, host=/var/run/postgresql,[REDACTED], postgresql:///db?host=/var/run/postgresql,evil.com — while single socket/loopback forms (host=/var/run/postgresql, host=/tmp, postgresql://localhost/db, host=127.0.0.1) ACCEPTED and UNC //evil/share still REJECTED. Test test_require_local_uri_rejects_multihost_socket_bypass (tests/test_persistence.py:186) passes (4 params). Discrimination CONFIRMED: I re-implemented the pre-fix behavior (no comma split, whole-value socket check) and all 4 bypass DSNs were ACCEPTED, meaning the test would fail if the split is reverted. All 21 related local-guard tests pass; no regression to the legitimate single-socket allowance.

Fix in src/assay_engine/methodology/confirm.py _validate_alpha (lines 83-91) now accepts alpha: float | None and rejects None with ValueError("alpha is required: lock it on the hypothesis ... or pass alpha= ...") BEFORE the 0.0 < alpha < 0.5 comparison that previously raised an opaque TypeError. Correct root cause (None resolution from _resolve_locked_param when alpha locked on neither hypothesis nor call), sound approach, and the right layer: _validate_alpha is the single shared validator on all three entry paths (verdict_from_pvalue line 141; confirm_unit_level resolves then routes through verdict_from_pvalue; confirm_whole_corpus resolves at line 370 and validates at line 380), so no path can reach a comparison with None. VERIFY reproduced: verdict_from_pvalue('h', statistic=1.0, p_value=0.01, alpha=None, powered=True, direction_supports_claim=True) raises ValueError matching 'alpha is required'. The regression test test_alpha_none_raises_clear_valueerror_not_opaque_typeerror (tests/test_confirm.py:132) DISCRIMINATES: it uses pytest.raises(ValueError, match='alpha is required'); TypeError is not a subclass of ValueError, so reverting the fix (letting 0.0 < None raise TypeError, confirmed at runtime) would not be caught and the test fails. Full suite: 45 passed, including all valid-alpha and the parametrized invalid-alpha (0.0/0.5/1.0) cases.

CI-1 is a genuine platform constraint, independently confirmed: `gh api repos/{owner}/{repo}/branches/main/protection` returns HTTP 403 ("Upgrade to GitHub Pro or make this repository public"), so required-status-check enforcement is impossible on this plan, and the two enabling paths (public repo, plan upgrade) are correctly judged out of scope (publishing transcripts / billing). Given that, a compensating control is the right and only in-scope response, and the implementation is sound at the right layer: (1) scripts/require_green_ci.sh is a real, working pre-merge gate — verified live, it exits 0 against green main and exits 1 against red harden/pass-7, uses set -euo pipefail and emits a ::error:: + nonzero on non-success; (2) docs/MERGE-POLICY.md documents the constraint, the silently-red mechanism, why enforcement is not enabled, and the mandatory manual procedure; (3) the ci.yml all-checks comment is honestly updated to flag that the gate is computed-but-unenforced (#CI-1) and warns against --admin merging past red. The regression test test_merge_gate_compensating_control_exists passes and DISCRIMINATES: removing the script makes it fail with an exists() AssertionError, and it independently asserts the policy doc and the #CI-1 ci.yml caveat. No residual gap that is fixable under the stated constraint.
concerns: Inherent residual (documented, not fixable under the constraint): the control is procedural — nothing mechanically forces require_green_ci.sh to run before merge (no git pre-push/pre-merge hook, no Makefile/CI wiring); it relies on operator discipline per MERGE-POLICY.md. Acceptable for a compensating control but worth a future hardening note.

Minor robustness gap: the script checks only the latest ci-workflow run's conclusion for the branch, not that the run's headSha matches the current branch tip — a stale green run from an earlier commit would pass the gate if no new run has completed for the latest push. Tightening to compare headSha would close this.

result.rationale: The platform constraint is real and unfixable in scope: hc-assay is genuinely PRIVATE and `gh api repos/{owner}/{repo}/branches/main/protection` returns a real HTTP 403 ("Upgrade to GitHub Pro or make this repository public"). Both platform fixes are legitimately out of scope (public → publishes transcripts; plan upgrade → billing). Given that, the compensating control is the correct layer and root cause: the remediation correctly diagnoses that `all-checks` is computed but not enforced — the exact mechanism that let CI go silently red across passes. scripts/require_green_ci.sh queries the latest ci.yml run conclusion and exits non-zero unless completed+success; since a red all-checks makes the whole run conclude failure, the script subsumes the intended gate. The ci.yml comment is now honest (#CI-1 caveat), and MERGE-POLICY.md documents the constraint and forbids --admin merges. Verified live: test passes; discriminates both ways (removing script fails at line 344, stripping #CI-1 fails at line 350); script exits 0 on green main (latest run=success) and exits 1 on red/no-run; all-checks fan-in job logic is sound; script committed executable (100755).
concerns: Non-blocking: the control is procedural and relies on someone actually running the script before merge — inherent to any compensating control for an unenforceable platform gate, and explicitly documented.
Non-blocking: an in-progress run and a failed run both yield exit 1; this is the safe-fail direction, not a gap.

result.rationale: The fix is appropriate in approach and layer: scripts/capture_transcripts.py:_hostname_patterns() correctly auto-derives the machine hostname (socket.gethostname/platform.node/scutil) and redacts bare+.local, while ASSAY_SCRUB_HOSTS namespace hosts get only their .local form redacted to preserve the bare project token. The >=4 length guard plus \b boundary properly avoid over-redacting short/common hostnames. The machine-hostname half is operationally PROVEN: git grep HEAD for [REDACTED] and [REDACTED] both return 0 in the committed corpus. The regression test (tests/test_scripts.py::test_scrub_redacts_machine_and_namespace_hostnames) passes and DISCRIMINATES — it monkeypatches socket/platform and rebuilds _HOSTNAME_RES, so if the patterns were reverted to empty the buildbox-7 / hc-proj.local assertions would fail; it also asserts bare hc-proj/app and settings.local.json are preserved.\n\nBUT it is NOT complete. The finding's explicit VERIFY claim ('grep transcripts/ for [REDACTED] -> 0' and 'The committed corpus was re-scrubbed') is FALSE. git grep HEAD finds [REDACTED] in 12 committed files (74 line-hits), including the genuine internal RDF namespace https://[REDACTED]/prov# — exactly the leak class the docstring says ASSAY_SCRUB_HOSTS exists to scrub. Root cause of the residual: the namespace-host (.local) redaction depends entirely on the ASSAY_SCRUB_HOSTS env var, which is set NOWHERE operationally (not in .githooks/pre-commit, no config, no wiring) — it appears only in the test (synthetic 'hc-proj') and the code's own docstring. So the .local half of the fix is effectively dead at runtime, and the named target [REDACTED] leaked straight into the very commit that claims to fix it.
concerns: VERIFY claim false: [REDACTED] is NOT 0 in the committed corpus. git grep HEAD -- transcripts/ finds it in 12 files / 74 line-hits, including the genuine internal RDF namespace https://[REDACTED]/prov# in agent-ac587049a13085463.jsonl, agent-afd8f190045689443.jsonl, agent-a492eed5034215bde.jsonl, the top-level session jsonl, and workflow .json files.

Namespace-host (.local) redaction is unwired at runtime: ASSAY_SCRUB_HOSTS is referenced only in scripts/capture_transcripts.py (code+docstring) and tests/test_scripts.py (synthetic 'hc-proj'). It is set nowhere in the pre-commit hook or any config, so _hostname_patterns() never receives 'hc-grc' in production and never redacts [REDACTED]. The named target of the finding is therefore not redacted by the actual capture path.

Corpus was not actually re-scrubbed for [REDACTED]: the operator-internal mDNS/namespace hostname remains in cleartext across the committed pass-9 transcripts despite the commit message asserting 'Re-scrubbed corpus (grep now 0)'. The machine-hostname half ([REDACTED]) genuinely works only because it is auto-derived without needing the env var.

Forward fix incomplete unless ASSAY_SCRUB_HOSTS=hc-grc (or equivalent) is wired into the pre-commit capture path AND the existing corpus is re-run; otherwise every future commit will continue to leak [REDACTED].

result.rationale:
The fix CODE is appropriate: _hostname_patterns() in scripts/capture_transcripts.py correctly derives the machine hostname (socket.gethostname/platform.node/scutil) and redacts its bare + .local forms, while ASSAY_SCRUB_HOSTS entries redact ONLY the .local FQDN form, preserving the bare project token. Verified empirically: with ASSAY_SCRUB_HOSTS=hc-grc, _scrub turns 'https://[REDACTED]/prov#' into 'https://[REDACTED]/prov#' while leaving bare 'hc-grc' intact. The short-hostname over-redaction guard (len(b) < 4 skip) is present. settings.local.json is untouched (no blanket *.local rule). Root cause, layer, and approach are all correct. The regression test test_scrub_redacts_machine_and_namespace_hostnames DISCRIMINATES: emptying _HOSTNAME_RES (simulating a revert) leaves both 'buildbox-7' and 'hc-proj.local' present, failing the assertions; the test passes with the fix in place.

HOWEVER the fix is INCOMPLETE. The finding's central completeness claim — 'The committed corpus was re-scrubbed' and 'VERIFY: grep transcripts/ for ... [REDACTED] -> 0' — is FALSE on branch harden/pass-9. grep finds 200 occurrences of [REDACTED] across 12 committed transcript files. While many are self-referential meta-discussion of this very finding, a substantial subset is the GENUINE infrastructure-host leak the finding was meant to remove: the PROV-O RDF namespace declaration '@prefix hcgrc: <https://[REDACTED]/prov#> .' read verbatim from a real project file and embedded in the corpus (51+ 'https://[REDACTED]/prov#' instances). The machine hostname [REDACTED] WAS fully scrubbed (0 bare, 0 .local), but [REDACTED] was not — strongly indicating the re-scrub ran WITHOUT ASSAY_SCRUB_HOSTS=hc-grc set, so the namespace-host rule produced no patterns for that corpus pass. The code works; the corpus was not actually re-scrubbed with the namespace host configured, leaving the targeted operator/infra host ([REDACTED]) still leaked in committed history.
concerns: Committed corpus still leaks the target host: grep transcripts/ for '[REDACTED]' returns 200 occurrences across 12 files on harden/pass-9, directly contradicting the finding's VERIFY claim of 0. The fix says 'The committed corpus was re-scrubbed' but it was not (or was re-scrubbed without ASSAY_SCRUB_HOSTS=hc-grc).

At least 51+ of these are a GENUINE infra leak, not meta-discussion: the RDF/PROV-O namespace declaration '@prefix hcgrc: <https://[REDACTED]/prov#> .' is embedded verbatim from a real project file (e.g. transcripts/.../wf_7a766754-e04.json) — exactly the internal-namespace host the finding aimed to scrub.

Asymmetry confirms incomplete re-scrub: [REDACTED] (machine hostname, auto-derived via scutil) shows 0 occurrences, but [REDACTED] (ASSAY_SCRUB_HOSTS-supplied) shows 200 — consistent with the re-scrub running without ASSAY_SCRUB_HOSTS set so the namespace rule emitted no patterns.

Test masks the real-machine scutil behavior: on this node scutil --get LocalHostName returns '[REDACTED]', so _hostname_patterns() appends a real [REDACTED] pattern during the test even though the test only monkeypatches socket.gethostname/platform.node to 'buildbox-7'. The test still discriminates on its asserted tokens, but it does not assert anything about scutil-derived hosts, so a regression in the scutil branch would not be caught.

result.rationale: Fix addresses the correct root cause (bare int()/float() of env vars raising an opaque 'invalid literal' that names no var, firing at import before any handler) at the right layer: a single shared src/assay_engine/_envparse.py with int_env/float_env that treat empty as default and raise a ValueError naming the var and value. All 8 seam.py env sites and all 4 checkpoint.py env sites are converted (grep confirms only int_env/float_env wrap ASSAY_* reads). The 3 remaining int(/float( in seam.py (lines 520/522/673) parse runtime request params, not env vars, and are correctly governed by a separate PermanentReasoningError contract — legitimately out of scope. Existing behavior preserved: empty/unset->default verified live, and the monkeypatched _MIGRATION_LOCK_TIMEOUT_MS fallback path is intact. The regression test test_numeric_env_parsing_names_the_bad_var passed (full file: 109 passed, 4 skipped) and DISCRIMINATES: reverting one seam.py site to int(os.environ.get(...)) makes the regex guard fail with a concrete match; the regex matches all reverted forms (int(os.environ, float(_env(, int(_env() while not false-positiving on the legitimate runtime-param parses. float_env naming and int_env naming both verified live.
concerns: Non-blocking: the named test asserts int_env's message directly but not float_env's ('must be a number'); I verified float_env naming works live, so this is coverage thinness, not a gap.
Non-blocking: the regex guard would miss an env value read into an intermediate local then parsed (x = os.environ[...]; int(x)); no such pattern exists in either file today.

result.rationale: P9-MO-2 is appropriate and complete within its declared scope (seam.py + checkpoint.py). Root cause correctly fixed: bare int()/float() of env vars raised an opaque 'invalid literal' naming no var; the new shared assay_engine._envparse (int_env/float_env) raises a ValueError naming the var, treats empty as default, and preserves (ValueError, TypeError) semantics. The max(...) clamps stay outside the parse so floor behavior is unchanged. Both files are fully migrated — grep shows no bare int(/float( wrapping os.environ or _env( remains; the three residual int(/float( in seam.py (lines 520/522/673) parse caller param dicts behind their own PermanentReasoningError contract and are correctly out of scope. All other os.environ reads in seam.py are string reads. Behavior verified at runtime: defaults, empty-string fallback, valid parse, var-named error, and the monkeypatched _MIGRATION_LOCK_TIMEOUT_MS path (including the new empty-string fall-through) all hold. The regression test DISCRIMINATES: I confirmed its static-assertion regex matches all three pre-fix forms (so reverting any one fails) while ignoring the out-of-scope param parses, and its runtime assertion checks the var name. test_numeric_env_parsing_names_the_bad_var plus the existing persistence/observability int_env tests all pass.
concerns: Minor / outside P9-MO-2 functional scope: the new _envparse.py module docstring overclaims it is 'the single source replacing the per-module copies that had grown in observability/tracing.py and persistence/vectorstore.py' — but both modules still define and use their own private _int_env (tracing.py:32, vectorstore.py:20). They were NOT migrated. Moreover vectorstore._int_env uses os.environ.get(name, default) so it does NOT treat an empty-string value as the default, unlike the shared int_env — so the copies are not even behaviorally identical. Either correct the docstring or migrate those two modules. This is a documentation accuracy issue, not a defect in the seam.py/checkpoint.py fix.
